Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic but it worked for me. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd usually block out time on weekends and maybe two evenings during the week when I could actually practice things, not just read about them. With limited resources at home, I often had to plan around when I could access better internet at the library or a friend's place. I'd write out physical schedules in a notebook because that helped me feel more in control of everything.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be super consistent with my 17 hours spread evenly, but other weeks everything would pile up toward the end. I work part-time to support myself, so sometimes shifts would throw off my whole plan and I'd have to cram studying into whatever gaps I could find. Deadlines were the main thing that shaped when I actually sat down to work.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check, like an assignment deadline or to see if grades were posted. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a discussion post or new material and that would prompt me to go look. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around, I needed a reason because my time was pretty limited between work and studies.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where logging in became more automatic for me, especially when the discussion forums were active and I actually wanted to see what classmates were saying. I'd check in most mornings with my coffee before heading out. But I wouldn't say it ever became a deep habit, more like something I did when the course felt alive and interactive. When things got quieter, I'd forget to check for days.
Q5: The video content was really valuable for me because I could watch demonstrations and actually see how things worked, which suits my learning style. I watched almost all of them, probably around 95 minutes worth. The discussion forums were also helpful when people shared practical examples or tips from their own experience. I learn better when I can connect concepts to real situations rather than just reading theory.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped or delayed the longer reading materials, especially PDFs that were just walls of text. They didn't stick with me the way hands-on activities or videos did. Some of the optional supplementary resources I barely touched because I had to prioritize what was going to directly help me with assignments and exams. When you're working with limited time and resources, you have to be strategic about what you engage with.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to something I'd be assessed on or when it helped me understand something I was genuinely confused about. If I could physically practice something or apply it somehow, that made it valuable. I also appreciated activities that didn't require me to be online for long stretches since my internet situation isn't always reliable.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different, like they were nice-to-haves rather than must-dos. I'd look at them and think about whether I had the bandwidth to engage, and usually the answer was no because I was focused on completing what was required. Sometimes I felt a bit guilty skipping them, but I had to be realistic about my energy and time. The required stuff always took priority because that's what affected my grade.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and materials when I had good internet access, then watch and review them later at home. I also took handwritten notes constantly and would practice problems on paper. I had a study group that met in person at the library, and we'd work through concepts together without any of us being logged into anything.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd print out lecture slides and annotate them by hand for hours, or when I'd be at the library with my study group working through practice problems together. I also did a lot of mental review while commuting to work, going over concepts in my head. The LMS only sees the tip of the iceberg of my actual learning.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work with them offline for days or even weeks. My internet at home isn't great and I couldn't afford to be streaming videos repeatedly, so downloading was essential. I'd come back to the same downloaded materials multiple times while preparing for exams without generating any new login activity.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially around midterms when my work schedule got crazy and I was exhausted. I remember one week where I barely logged in at all because I was pulling extra shifts and just couldn't focus on academics. The mindfulness exercises they offered actually helped me get through some of those rough patches, helping me reset mentally when everything felt overwhelming.
Q13: Definitely, all of those factors played a role at different times. When I was tired from work, I'd log in but not really absorb anything, just going through the motions. Confusion sometimes made me avoid certain topics rather than face them head-on. Competing responsibilities were probably the biggest factor though, I had to constantly balance studying with earning enough money to pay my bills, and sometimes work won that battle.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd be logged in and scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Maybe I was worried about money or tired from a long shift, and I'd realize I'd been staring at the same page for ten minutes without taking anything in. Those sessions probably showed up as engagement in the system, but they weren't productive at all. Quality of engagement matters more than just being present.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they were important, which is probably why my assignment completion rate wasn't great. Sometimes it was because I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start. Other times I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure, which wasn't always true. The assignments I avoided were usually the ones that seemed most intimidating or required resources I wasn't sure I had access to.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'd be logging in much more frequently, watching videos I'd been putting off, checking forums for hints. My engagement was very deadline-driven, which isn't ideal but it's how I functioned given everything else going on. Without deadlines, I probably would have been even less active online.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, especially positive feedback which motivated me to keep going. When I got good marks on something, I'd feel more confident tackling the next task. Constructive criticism was helpful too, though sometimes it took me a while to act on it because I'd need to process it emotionally first. I appreciated when feedback came quickly because then I could adjust my approach while the material was still fresh.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed because I'm not naturally super organized. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me pace myself, even if I didn't always stick to the intended timeline. The structure also made it easier to identify what was most important when I had to triage my time. I appreciated that it wasn't too rigid, allowing some flexibility for people like me with complicated schedules.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier for me given my internet situation. The discussion forums being optional but encouraged was good because I could participate when I had energy without feeling forced. What made it harder was when activities required being online for extended periods or when instructions weren't clear about what exactly was expected. Ambiguity always slowed me down.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets the standards. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, trying to learn, but when it comes to assignments they freeze up or run out of time. It could also be someone dealing with personal issues who's trying to stay connected to the course even when they can't complete everything. High activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience pretty well. I got a Merit despite not having super high LMS engagement because a lot of my real learning happened offline. I studied effectively with downloaded materials, practiced on paper, and worked with my study group in person. The exam score shows I learned the material even if the LMS didn't capture all my effort. Some of us just learn better outside of digital platforms.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone is most active, what materials they access, and how often they participate in discussions. That's useful for identifying students who might be disengaging completely. But logs miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually absorbing material or just clicking through. They also miss all offline studying, group work, and the mental processing that happens away from the screen. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. For me, it's when I'm curious about a topic and want to dig deeper, when I'm making connections between concepts, when I'm discussing ideas with classmates. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged in. You can be technically present without being truly engaged, and you can be deeply engaged without much visible activity.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I grew in confidence throughout this course, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage stress during tough weeks. It wouldn't capture my study group sessions where we taught each other concepts, or the hours I spent practicing with downloaded materials at home. It also wouldn't show the personal challenges I overcame just to stay in the course, working while studying, dealing with limited resources, and still managing to earn a Merit. That resilience doesn't show up in login data.
